coredata.dc:title: Comparative study of machine learning techniques for the state of health estimation of Li-Ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

coredata.dc:creator: Eleftheriadis, Panagiotis
Gangi, Manfredi
Leva, Sonia
Rey, Alberto Valdes
Groppo, Emanuele
Grande, Lorenzo
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-Ion batteries play a crucial role in vehicle electrification to meet the goals of reducing fossil fuels. However, they deteriorate over time and it is thus needed to predict their rate of decay since, after a certain threshold, they are not suitable anymore for their designed application. One of the main indicators that displays the aging status of a cell is the State of Health (SOH), which expresses the remaining total capacity of the cell in comparison with its initial total capacity. For an accurate evaluation of the SOH, various methods have been proposed in the literature. In this article, a comparative study of nine data-driven techniques in terms of error and processing time is presented. These different algorithms have been tested on two public datasets, extracting selected features and performing an Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA) to estimate the SOH of a cell using only partial discharges. The benefits of using Machine Learning (ML) methods for the models can be found in the processing times that are faster than experimental methods, making the entire industrial testing stage faster.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Data-driven
Deep learning
Machine learning
State of health

Long-short term memory recurrent Neural Network
Nonlinear autoregressive network with exogenous inputs
Long-Short Term Memory Recurrent Neural Network

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the algorithm procedure. The different steps have been processed independently for each dataset.
Voltage profiles of the Oxford used cells during all the cycles. The red lines indicate the voltage range of the profiles used for the data processing.
Discharge capacity after each cycle. Not all the cells went through the same testing since Cells 4 and 6 were disconnected with respect to the others.
Voltage profiles of the NASA used cells during all the cycles. The red lines indicate the voltage range of the profiles used for the data processing.
Discharge capacity after each cycle of the NASA dataset. The spikes are due to the rest of the cell after each cycle.
Oxford cells ICA curves. The red dot indicates the peak at each cycle.
NASA cells ICA curves. The red dot indicates the peak at each cycle.
(a) Voltage associated with the IC curve peak of Oxford Dataset. (b) Voltage associated with the IC curve peak of NASA Dataset.
(a) Area of each ICA curve at each cycle for the Oxford dataset. (b) Area of each ICA curve at each cycle for the NASA dataset.
(a) Summary of the features extracted from a typical ICA curve for the Oxford dataset. (b) Summary of the features extracted from a typical ICA curve for the NASA dataset.
Schematic representation of a regression tree.
Schematic representation of an ensemble regression tree.
Principle of the Support Vector Regression.
Application of the Gaussian process regression.
An example of a Feed Forward Neural Network for the SOH estimation.
An example of a Convolutional Neural Network for the SOH estimation.
An example of the nonlinear autoregressive network with exogenous inputs in a closed configuration for the SOH estimation.
An example of the long-short term memory cell for the SOH estimation.
(a) SOH Estimation of cell 8 from the Oxford dataset through Regression Tree method. (b) SOH Estimation of cell 3 from the NASA dataset through the Regression Tree method.
(a) SOH Estimation of cell 8 from the Oxford dataset through Ensemble Tree method. (b) SOH Estimation of cell 3 from the NASA dataset through the Ensemble Tree method.
(a) SOH Estimation of cell 8 from the Oxford dataset through Support Vector Regression method. (b) SOH Estimation of cell 3 from the NASA dataset through Support Vector Regression method.
(a) SOH Estimation of cell 8 from the Oxford dataset through GLM method. (b) SOH Estimation of cell 3 from the NASA dataset through GLM method.
(a) SOH Estimation of cell 8 from the Oxford dataset through GPR method. (b) SOH Estimation of cell 3 from the NASA dataset through GPR method.
(a) SOH Estimation of cell 8 from the Oxford dataset through the Feed Forward Neural Network method. (b) SOH Estimation of cell 3 from the NASA dataset through the Feed Forward Neural Network method.
(a) SOH Estimation of cell 8 from the Oxford dataset through CNN method. (b) SOH Estimation of cell 3 from the NASA dataset through CNN method.
(a) SOH Estimation of cell 8 from the Oxford dataset through NARX method. (b) SOH Estimation of cell 3 from the NASA dataset through NARX method.
(a) SOH Estimation of cell 8 from the Oxford dataset through LSTM method. (b) SOH Estimation of cell 3 from the NASA dataset through LSTM method.
Overview of all the SOH predictions obtained for the Oxford dataset.
Overview of all the SOH predictions obtained for the NASA dataset.
Evaluation metrics comparison of all models for the Oxford dataset.
Evaluation metrics comparison of all models for the NASA dataset.
Comparing the models that exhibit the three lowest RMSE values for the Oxford dataset.
Comparing the models that exhibit the three lowest RMSE values for the NASA dataset.
Specifications of the battery cells.
Model and distribution options for the generalized linear model.
Model and distribution options for the Gaussian process regression.
Hyperspace of the feedforward Neural Network model.
Hyperspace of the convolutional Neural Network model.
Hyperspace of the long-short term memory recurrent neural network.
RMSE of the estimated SOH for each model for the two employed datasets. In bold the best 3 models for each dataset.
Processing time for each model for training and estimation.

head.ce:title: Comparative study of machine learning techniques for the state of health estimation of Li-Ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Politecnico di Milano, Via Lambruscini 4A, Milan, 20156, Lombardia, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Politecnico di Milano, addressline=Via Lambruscini 4A, city=Milan, postcode=20156, state=Lombardia, country=Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Politecnico di Torino, Torino, Piedmont, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Politecnico di Torino, city=Torino, state=Piedmont, country=Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-Ion batteries play a crucial role in vehicle electrification to meet the goals of reducing fossil fuels. However, they deteriorate over time and it is thus needed to predict their rate of decay since, after a certain threshold, they are not suitable anymore for their designed application. One of the main indicators that displays the aging status of a cell is the State of Health (SOH), which expresses the remaining total capacity of the cell in comparison with its initial total capacity. For an accurate evaluation of the SOH, various methods have been proposed in the literature. In this article, a comparative study of nine data-driven techniques in terms of error and processing time is presented. These different algorithms have been tested on two public datasets, extracting selected features and performing an Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA) to estimate the SOH of a cell using only partial discharges. The benefits of using Machine Learning (ML) methods for the models can be found in the processing times that are faster than experimental methods, making the entire industrial testing stage faster.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to address the problems related to climate change, governments are pushing to issue new rules and directives [1], introducing plans for the electrification of all fossil fuel-dependent sectors, such as mobility. Moreover, the current high need for new energy storage systems to support renewable integration provides an opportunity for the battery industry to grow, especially because of the performance and rapid development of Lithium-Ion Batteries (LIBs) [2]. One of the most relevant problems of LIB is that their performance decays with time and usage due to the deterioration of their electrochemical constituents, resulting in capacity and power fade [3]. This phenomenon is called battery aging and it is influenced by both internal factors, such as battery chemistry and manufacturing, as well as environmental and operating conditions [4]. Due to degradation, after some time batteries fail to meet their intended energy requirements, and they reach the so-called End Of Life (EOL) since they are not suitable anymore for their first-life application. After the EOL, the battery pack can be either recycled or repurposed, forming Second Life Battery (SLB) products [5]. To this end, health diagnostic and prognostic tools are required to ensure the safety and reliability of batteries despite aging. Two crucial indicators that give information regarding the state of the battery cell are the State of Charge (SOC) and State of Health (SOH), where both parameters are correlated [6]. SOC and SOH estimation techniques have been developed to track the actual performance of batteries in operation [6,7]. The SOH indicates the current capability of a battery to store and supply energy compared to the one at the beginning of its life, while the SOC shows the percentage of charge present in the battery with respect to its possible maximum in a specific time instant. This article focuses only on the SOH, comparing different techniques to predict battery capacity after several discharging cycles.

To estimate the SOH of a battery it is possible to adopt either experimental methods, model-based methods, or Machine Learning (ML) algorithms [8]. The main problem related to the first two categories is that the LIB degradation mechanisms are very complex to model due to the nonlinearity of the phenomenon [9], so physics-based models are not so reliable to estimate the SOH [10]. Given the aforementioned issues, the exploitation of data-driven methods based on machine learning algorithms is becoming more popular considering that it is not necessary to rely on a battery model [11]. In literature, various studies have employed different methods for SOH estimation using datasets that comprise either full or partial charging and discharging cycles, as well as various input features. In [12], the authors employed Support Vector Machine (SVM), Gaussian Process Regression (GPR), and Relevance Vector Machine (RVM) on open source datasets [13,14] to estimate the SOH. They extracted capacity and capacity difference as features using partial cycles and achieved a minimum test RMSE of 0.29%, without mentioning the duration of the processing time. Another interesting analysis has been carried out in [15], where Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) have been used with the NASA dataset [16], obtaining an estimation with an RMSE of 0.035. In [17], LSTM and Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) were used together by the authors, who were able to obtain a mean absolute percentage error of 0.76% using a hybrid LSTM-RNN model applied to the full cycles from the open source dataset of [18]. Furthermore, the Feed Forward Neural Network (FNN), the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and the LSTM models have been used in [19], where the analysis focused on both the single method implementation and the usage of multi-channel techniques on NASA dataset, demonstrating that the last ones improve the overall estimation process. A further valuable model is the Non-Linear Autoregressive Neural Network (NARX) which has been used in [20], where the analysis involved both testings on full cycles and partial cycles, achieving an RMSE of 0.26% in the extracting health indicators from partial charging. Acknowledging that carrying out full charging and discharging cycles may not be viable in the EV industry [11], the most favorable approaches could be developed using partial data.

The expected outcome of the present paper is to compare different data-driven methods, among them the ones mentioned in the literature, in order to see which performs better both in terms of processing time and accuracy using partial charge and discharge data so that in future studies it will be easier to either identify the most suitable algorithm to implement and the best features to insert in the ML models. To this end, two different open-source datasets have been selected from which information regarding voltage, current and capacity of different cells have been extracted and then processed by doing an Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA). This technique allows transforming voltage plateaux on the charge/discharge curves into peaks on the incremental capacity (IC) curve. This transformation is achieved by differentiating the charge/discharge capacity with respect to the terminal voltage. ICA might work with data from a single or few batteries, and it is usable for online SOH estimation: some studies have already been carried out to extend the obtained results at the pack level [21,22]. However, a drawback is that this method is chemistry-dependent, so it cannot be considered general, and a fitting process is required to obtain readable curves. In Fig. 1 it is presented the general overview of the procedure followed for this study. In summary, the main contributions of this analysis are:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Comparison in terms of accuracy and training and estimation time for the SOH estimation for nine data-driven techniques.
Utilization of two public datasets for the comparison of the data-driven techniques enhancing the reproducibility and interpretability of the results.
Use of input features based on the ICA, a well-established parameter for the estimation of health indicators of LIB batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: This article is organized into different sections. In particular, in Section 2 it is possible to find a description of the two used datasets, followed by the selection of the features to be extracted from the ICA, which are the inputs for the data-driven algorithms. Then, in Section 3 it presents a theoretical evaluation of all the methods considered for this comparative study. Following, Section 4 presents the final results of the SOH estimation, while Section 5 contains information regarding the adopted metric and the final comparison of the data-driven models. Finally, Section 6 reports the final conclusions.

Among the open-source datasets available online, two datasets were used for this study. The first one provided by Oxford University has been chosen [23,24], where battery aging data have been collected from 8 Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC) pouch cells, tested in parallel in a thermal chamber at 40°C. The nominal capacity of the cells is 740 mAh, the nominal voltage 3.7V, the charge voltage limit 4.2V and the cut-off voltage 2.7V. The cells have been exposed to a constant-current, constant-voltage charging profile, followed by a drive cycle discharging profile obtained from the urban Artemis profile. Characterization measurements have been taken every 100 cycles in 4 different conditions, consisting of charge and discharge at 1C and at C/18.5 (the rate that the authors used to obtain the pseudo-OCV relationship), with further detailed info provided in the supporting information file available with the data. In Fig. 2 it is possible to see the voltage profiles of all the cells of the dataset and the aging mechanism is easy to recognize given the reduction of the discharging time. The aging phenomenon is also visible pointing at the maximum charge of every cell after each cycle in Fig. 3 where a downward trend is present as expected. In the following analysis, data associated with the discharging phase are shown and used, since are more interesting from a practical perspective. However, the behavior during the charging phase can be modeled as well, yielding similar results. For the Oxford dataset, discharge cycles at 1C are considered, for the feature extraction whereas pseudo-OCV measurements have been used as a reference for the nominal capacity value. The voltage at the cell terminals decreases during discharge, always in the range 2.7 V to 4.2 V as mentioned before. Nevertheless, given the application of this method for the prediction of SOH in an industrial pipeline, it is preferred that the voltage range be optimized to reduce the processing time for a single battery. To achieve such a result, a smaller range has been considered between 3.4V and 4.1V, where the highest peak of the ICA plot is situated, as suggested in [25]. During discharge at 1C, the cell is discharged with a rated current of 750 mAh. At the initial state, SOH of 100%, the capacity that can be extracted from the battery is the rated one of 740 mAh.

As a second database, the dataset from the NASA Prognostics Center of Excellence Data Set Repository was used [16], where the dataset consists of 4 LIB cells, the #5, #6, #7 and #18 with a nominal capacity of 2 Ah, maximum charge voltage 4.2V, and cut-off voltage 2.7V, 2.6V, 2.2V and 2.5V for the cells #5, #6, #7 and #18 respectively. For coding purposes, these cells have been renamed cell1, cell2, cell3 and cell4 respectively. In Fig. 4 it is possible to see the voltage profile at each discharge cycle for all four cells of the dataset. With respect to the previous dataset, from the NASA cells voltage, it can be seen from the plots that the profiles are less uniform that is a good point since it makes it possible to see how the ML models answer to different data structures.

Fig. 5 shows the discharge capacity of each cell and the degradation phenomenon is clear since the plots tend to decrease. Then, in Table 1 it is possible to see an overview of all the information regarding the cells used in both Oxford and NASA datasets.
In the next part of the article, a description of which features have been extracted from the datasets for the SOH estimation analysis will be presented.

The datasets mentioned before were preprocessed and three features were extracted from the discharge profiles of each cycle. All of the features are information related to the ICA curve, the highest value of the ICA curve, the corresponding voltage value, and the area of the ICA curve. To form the ICA curve, first, the Incremental Capacity (IC) is determined as:
(1)
IC =
d Q
d V
where the
d Q
is the change in capacity and
d V
is the corresponding change in voltage. This value is plotted against the cell voltage to obtain the ICA curve, which is then processed with the Savitzky–Golay filter to smooth away the spikes from the curve and make it suitable for feature extraction. In Fig. 6 are presented the voltage and discharge capacity on the left, which were used to obtain the IC, and the ICA curve on the right.

Among the different features that could be extracted from the ICA curve, the one with the best predictive information is the highest peak of the ICA curve [21,26]. During the degradation process of cycling, these peaks tend to be smaller in magnitude, while they are shifted to lower voltage values. Hence, the highest peak of the ICA curve, which is shown in Fig. 7 for the Oxford dataset and in Fig. 8 for the NASA dataset, the associated voltage shown in Fig. 9 and the area of each ICA shown in Fig. 10 for both datasets were selected as features for the model. In those figures, it can be seen both the decreasing behavior of the highest peak of the ICA curve and the decreasing corresponding voltage value.

As it can be seen in Fig. 6, the ICA curve has more than one peak that could be used as features for the model, although while aging, those peaks in the Oxford dataset tend to disappear, thus, they are not considered. Data-driven algorithms are prone to work better as more significant features are included. Accordingly, the area of the ICA curve was selected as an extra feature, which encodes the information on the variation of all peaks of the curve. Figs. 7 and 8 present the value of the area of all Oxford cells, with the same decreasing behavior while aging the battery cells. The three extracted features for each ICA curve can be graphically visualized in Fig. 11.

For the Oxford dataset, cells 1 to 6 have been selected for the training phase, while cell 7 for the validation of the ML methods and cell 8 for the final testing phase. In the same way, for the NASA dataset, consisting of fewer cells, cells 1 and 2 were used for the training phase, cell 4 for the validation stage, and cell 3 for the final testing step.

As previously stated, several data-driven models have been utilized to forecast the SOH of a Li-Ion battery. In this research, the predictive capability of various models such as Regression Tree, Ensemble Tree, Generalized Linear Model (GLM), Gaussian Process Regression (GPR), Support Vector Regression (SVR), Feed-forward Neural Network (FNN), Non-linear Autoregressive Network with Exogenous Inputs (NARX), Long-Short Term Memory Recurrent Neural Network (LSTM), and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) were evaluated. The following subsections provide a brief description of those models.

The Regression Trees are simple structures, based on the decision trees, but instead of the target values being class labels in leaves, they are continuous values. Regression trees employ a modified approach to selecting splits, whereby the input variables are utilized to divide the data into smaller and more homogeneous subsets, and in this particular case, the binary splits for regression. As it can be seen in Fig. 12, the schematic representation of a regression tree would look like a hierarchical structure, with the single root node at the top representing the entire dataset, and each subsequent split represented by a branch that leads to a child node, while each child node represents a subset of the data, and the split criterion for that node is shown along the branch. At the bottom of the tree, the leaf nodes represent the predicted value of the SOH for data points that fall into that subset. For this study, the MATLAB library of the Regression Tree was used [27], with the automatic hyperparameter optimization option.

The Ensemble Tree is an assemblage of regression trees where the forecast model comprises a weighted aggregation of multiple regression trees, as it can be seen in Fig. 13, enhancing the predictive accuracy. For this study, the MATLAB library of the Ensemble Tree was used [28], with a hyperparameter optimization option of the number of learning cycles, the learning rate, and the maximum number of splits.

The Support Vector Regression is a commonly used data-driven technique based on the same principle as the Support Vector Machine (SVM). SVM regression is classified as a non-parametric method as it depends on kernel functions. The fundamental concept behind SVR is to obtain the optimal fitting line, which is achieved by identifying the hyperplane that encompasses the maximum number of data points. The overall concept is generalized in Fig. 14. Unlike with other regression models, SVR endeavors to fit the most suitable line within a specific threshold value, than minimizing the error between the predicting and the real value. The SVR is composed of three main parts:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The Hyperplane, which represents the decision boundaries that are defined to predict the output value of the model.

The Kernel, which represents the mathematical function required to establish the hyperplanes and convert the input data into the predicted value. The kernel could be linear, non-linear, polynomial, radial basis function, etc.
The Boundary Lines, which represent the threshold value around the hyperplane that the SVR tries to fit.

ce:section.ce:para: For this study, the MATLAB library of the SVR was used [29], with the automatic hyperparameter optimization option.

The Generalized Linear Model is a general category of models, where there is at least one linear correlation between one or more predictive terms and the response. In those models, it is presupposed that the dependent variable
y
i
conforms to a probability distribution of the exponential family with mean
μ
i
which is often a nonlinear function of the covariates. The GLM is composed of three components:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Random Component, which represents the probability distribution of the dependent variable. In the case of a classical regression model, the random component corresponds to the normal distribution, while in the case of the binary logistic regression, the binomial distribution. In other cases, the component can be the Poisson, gamma, or inverse Gaussian.
Systematic Component, which represents the independent variables in the model, specifically, their linear combination of predictors denoted by X .

Link Function f , which represents the connection between the random and the systematic elements, determining how the predicted value of the dependent variable is linked to the linear combination of independent variables, defining the model as
f
(
μ
i
)
= X b

ce:section.ce:para: For this study, the MATLAB library of the GLM was used [30], while as the optimized parameters are influenced by both the selected model and distribution, a grid search technique has been employed to determine the most suitable pairs. In Table 2 it can be seen the options for the model and the distribution.

The GPR is a Bayesian, non-parametric kernel-based regression technique that tends to perform well on small datasets and provide uncertainty estimates for its predictions. Recently, its high accuracy has led to a surge of interest in utilizing it for data-driven estimation. As a non-parametric method, GPR is not restricted by a specific functional form but instead calculates the probability distribution over all possible functions that fit the data. Firstly, a prior on the function space is specified, while the posterior is then evaluated using the training data, and finally, the predictive posterior distribution is computed for the points of interest. In Fig. 15 it can be seen the GPR evaluation procedure explained previously.

For this study, the MATLAB library of the GPR was used [31], while as the optimized parameters are influenced by both the selected model Kernel and the Basis function, a grid search technique has been employed to determine the most suitable pairs. In Table 3 it can be seen the options for the model and the distribution, while other parameters of the model were optimized automatically.

Among the huge category of Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), the FNN is considered the first and the most basic network. It consists of the Input layer, the Hidden layers, and the Output layer, where the information moves only in the forward direction from the input layer, through the hidden layers, and to the output layer, as it can be seen in Fig. 16. In this architecture, every neuron in a given layer is linked to all the neurons in the succeeding layer. When there is a single hidden layer in a neural network, it is referred to as a Single Layer Perceptron, whereas a neural network with more than one hidden layer is called a Multi-Layer Perceptron.

For this study, the MATLAB library of the FNN was used [32], while as the optimized parameters are influenced by both the selected number of neurons of the hidden layer and the activation functions of the layers, a grid search technique has been employed to determine the most suitable pairs. The available choices for the hyperparameters mentioned can be observed in Table 4.

The CNN is a more advanced type of Artificial Neural Network (ANN) that has become widely used in computer vision due to its sophistication. The CNN has the same architecture of input, hidden, and output layers, but instead of general matrix multiplications between the layers, CNN uses a mathematical operation known as convolution. Generally, a convolutional layer involves a dot product operation between the input matrix and convolution kernel using the Frobenius inner product, and the activation function is often ReLU. The convolution operation produces a feature map as the kernel slides over the input matrix, which is then used as input for the subsequent layer. This is typically followed by additional layers such as pooling, flatten, fully connected, and normalization layers. In Fig. 17 it can be seen as an example of a CNN for the SOH estimation.

For this study, the MATLAB library of the CNN was used [33], while as the optimized parameters are influenced by the selected number of filters of the convolutional layers a grid search technique has been employed to determine the most suitable pairs. In Table 5 it can be seen the options for the aforementioned hyperparameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nonlinear autoregressive network with exogenous inputs
ce:section.ce:para:
Breaking the forward way of moving the information in the FNN, the NARX network is a type of recurrent dynamic network that incorporates feedback connections across multiple layers. This particular network architecture is capable of learning how to forecast a single time series by utilizing its previous values, feedback input, and an additional time series referred to as the external time series. The general architecture of the NARX model is presented in Fig. 18, where the
n _ l
,
n _ F
and
n _ L a y e r s
represent the input delay, the feedback delay, and the number of units of the hidden layer, respectively.

For this study, the MATLAB library of the NARX was used [34], while as the optimized parameters are influenced by the number of neurons of the hidden layer, a grid search technique has been employed to determine the most suitable pairs. Through a process of trial and error, it was observed that incorporating the input delay and feedback delay did not yield improved outcomes. Consequently, these variables were not incorporated into the hyperspace. A range of 10 to 90 with an increment of 5 was selected as the search space for the number of hidden layers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-short term memory recurrent Neural Network
ce:section.ce:para: The LSTM is a type of RNN where information from the past and the future states of the cell are utilized to better capture the dynamics of the model. The LSTM cell is composed of the forget, the input, and the output gate as it can be seen in Fig. 19. The forget gate is utilized to delete information from the memory, the input gate is to add information to the memory, and the output gate passes the information to the next cell which when is the last cell, the output of the model. In Fig. 19 is presented the model architecture for the SOH estimation with a sequential connection of the input layer, the LSTM layer, and the fully connected layer to generate the estimated SOH value.

For this study, the MATLAB library of the LSTM was used [35], while as the optimized parameters are influenced by both the selected number of units of the LSTM layer and the number of neurons of the FC layer, a grid search technique has been employed to determine the most suitable pairs. In Table 6 it can be seen the options for the aforementioned hyperparameters.
The final results that have been obtained for each method are reported in this section through the plots of the estimated SOH, while in the following section, the error metric was introduced.

In Fig. 20 it is possible to see the results for the SOH estimation for the Oxford and NASA datasets, where it can be noticed that the algorithm works better in the first dataset since there are more observations with respect to the first dataset with fewer spikes present.

In Fig. 21 it is possible to see the results for the SOH estimation for the Oxford and NASA datasets, where the results are excellent for the first dataset given the good amount of input data and the non-linearity dependence with the outputs, which makes the model works better than in the second dataset. The optimization procedure for the Oxford dataset yielded the following hyperparameters: 498 learning cycles, 0.076 learning rate, and 2 maximum number of splits. In addition, through the optimization procedure carried out on the NASA dataset, the hyperparameters determined were as follows: 102 learning cycles, 0.0788 learning rate, and 339 maximum number of splits.

In Fig. 22 it is possible to see the results for the SOH estimation for the Oxford and NASA datasets, where the result is quite good for both cases since SVR can work well also when there are not a lot of samples and the relationship among the data shows a non-linear trend.

In Fig. 23 it is possible to see the results for the SOH estimation for the Oxford and NASA datasets, where the results are not particularly good in both cases since GLM works better if used to estimate linear phenomenon. The optimization procedure for the Oxford dataset yielded the following hyperparameters: a Pure Quadratic model and a Normal distribution. In addition, through the optimization procedure carried out on the NASA dataset, the hyperparameters determined were as follows: an Interactions model and a Poisson distribution.

In Fig. 24 it is possible to see the results for the SOH estimation for the Oxford and NASA datasets. In this case, the results are both good since GPR works better when it is used to analyze non-linear phenomena, and it remains robust even if there is not too much data as input. The optimization procedure for the Oxford dataset yielded the following hyperparameters: a Rational Quadratic kernel model, None distribution, and a sigma of 1.443. In addition, through the optimization procedure carried out on the NASA dataset, the hyperparameters determined were as follows: an Exponential kernel model, a None distribution, and a sigma of 143.201.

In Fig. 25 it is possible to see the results for the SOH estimation for the Oxford and NASA datasets. The benefit of using FNN is that the model works well when it is used to describe complex non-linear relationships, such as battery aging, and it tends to work well when there is more input data. As a matter of fact, the estimation made with the Oxford dataset is better with respect to one of the NASA dataset since the first one has more inputs. The optimization procedure for the Oxford dataset yielded the following hyperparameters: a hidden layer with 10 neurons, a sigmoid activation function for the hidden layer, and a linear activation function for the output layer. In addition, through the optimization procedure carried out on the Oxford dataset, the hyperparameters determined were as follows: a hidden layer consisting of 50 neurons, a sigmoid activation function applied to the hidden layer, and a tanh activation function applied to the output layer.

In Fig. 26 it is possible to see the results for the SOH estimation for the Oxford and NASA datasets. Even though being an algorithm mainly used for image classification, CNN shows good enough results in terms of accuracy in the estimation considering both datasets. The optimization procedure for both the Oxford dataset and NASA dataset yielded the same hyperparameters: 30 filters were applied to the first convolutional layer, and 5 filters were used for the second convolutional layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nonlinear autoregressive network with exogenous inputs
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 27 it is possible to see the results for the SOH estimation for the Oxford and NASA datasets. Even though the method is used to work well with non-linear phenomena, it is more suited for time series prediction instead of single feature analysis, so the accuracy of the estimation is not excellent. The optimization procedure for the Oxford dataset yielded a hidden layer with 20 neurons, while for the NASA dataset a hidden layer with 30 neurons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-Short Term Memory Recurrent Neural Network
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 28 it is possible to see the results for the SOH estimation for the Oxford and NASA datasets. The results obtained for the cell of the Oxford dataset are better since LSTM requires a large amount of data to train effectively since there are numerous parameters that the network needs to adjust from the data and, while if the dataset is too small, the risk is to do not have enough information to calculate the architecture’s parameters. The optimization procedure for the Oxford dataset yielded the following hyperparameters: an LSTM layer with 20 units, and a hidden layer consisting of 30 neurons. In addition, through the optimization procedure carried out on the Oxford dataset, the hyperparameters determined were as follows: an LSTM layer with 20 units, and a hidden layer consisting of 20 neurons.

All the predictions are plotted together in Fig. 29 for the Oxford dataset, and in Fig. 30 regarding the NASA one. To better compare the methods, in the following an evaluation metric will be introduced, followed by some considerations regarding the processing time of each method, which is a fundamental parameter to be considered in the industrial sector.

Three different evaluation metrics have been selected to assess the predictive capabilities of each model. Specifically, the metrics used in the analysis are Root-Mean-Squared Error (RMSE), Mean Average Error (MAE), and Mean Average Percentage Error (MAPE). The first comparative index considered is RMSE, which is defined as:
(2)
RMSE =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
( S O
H
i
−
S O
H
i
ˆ
)
2
where the
S O
H
i
represents the reference value of SOH in the
i
t h
cycle, calculated using the first pseudo-OCV cycle, while the
S O
H
i
ˆ
represents the predicted value of SOH in the
i
t h
cycle, generated by each model. One of the main benefits of using RMSE is that it is very sensitive to outliers as it squares the errors, so large errors have a disproportionately large effect on the RMSE final value. The second evaluation metric used is the MAE, which is calculated as:
(3)
M A E =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
| S O
H
i
−
S O H
ˆ
i
|
where
S O
H
i
and
S O
H
i
ˆ
have already previously introduced as parameters. MAE is less sensitive with respect to the RMSE since it does not square the errors. The last evaluation index introduced in this study is the MAPE, which measures the percentage difference between predicted and observed values relative to the observed values. MAPE is defined as:
(4)
MAPE =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
|
S O
H
i
−
S O H
ˆ
i
S O
H
i
|
× 100 %
The deployed models were simulated on a computer equipped with an Intel i-910900KF 3.70 GHz processor, and 64 GB DDR4 memory. The main results in terms of SOH prediction and associated error are presented here for each of the investigated models. In Fig. 31 are presented the results of the metrics obtained for each model for the Oxford dataset, showing the best predictive capabilities of the FNN model, followed by the GPR and Ensemble Tree models. In Fig. 32 are presented the results for each model for the NASA dataset, and it is possible to see that the lowest errors are obtained using the GPR model, followed by the FNN and CNN model. In Table 7 it is possible to see the numerical values associated of each metrics associated to each algorithms used for the estimation of SOH for all models for both Oxford and NASA datasets.

Apart from the three aforementioned evaluation metrics calculated before, for a more multilevel comparison of the models, four additional parameters have been identified: the Time to Tune, the Interpretability, the Simplicity, and the Flexibility. The first one is the total processing time as the sum of train and test time, which are reported in Table 8 and, along with the three error metrics, they are quantitative indicators since their scores come from the actual values obtained during the analysis, while the last three are qualitative terms that have been evaluated using subjective experience and heuristics obtained from the literature. Interpretability indicates the degree to which a human can understand and explain the behavior of a data-driven algorithm [36]. Flexibility refers to the capability of a model to be updated and to work with big data. Lastly, the fifth index named Simplicity highlights the complexity of the algorithm in terms of the number of parameters and the value range of each parameter.

In Figs. 33 and 34 for both datasets, the three methods with the lowest RMSE have been reported and compared, considering the aforementioned parameters. Indeed, even if the RMSE is one of the most relevant indicators to consider during a model’s selection process, it is also fundamental to consider the time needed to tune the algorithm. For instance, in Fig. 33 GPR is one of the methods with the best accuracy but at the same time it is also the most demanding in terms of processing time. Hence, it may be more advantageous to sacrifice a certain degree of accuracy to enhance the processing time’s efficiency and, considering an industrial context, reduce the total testing cost. That being stated, when considering the Oxford dataset, it takes 7.06 s to train the FNN model, resulting in an RMSE of 0.250 for the Oxford dataset and 0.577 for the NASA dataset, making this model the most efficient in this case since also the total processing time is approximately 50 times less respect to other efficient algorithms such as GPR and LSTM. Therefore, it could be more beneficial to utilize the FNN model, which is considered simpler than the LSTM model, even though it may sacrifice some degree of flexibility. This decision, however, ultimately depends on the specific application being considered.

A detailed comparison in terms of RMSE, MAE and MAPE metrics has been carried out between several possible ML and data-driven techniques for the SOH estimation of Li-Ion cells considering two different datasets with distinct characteristics. A set of hyperparameters was identified for each model, without an exhaustive analysis and with an equal number of iterations of the optimization method to ensure the comparability of the results. Using two public datasets, three input features were utilized, the maximum value of the ICA, the corresponding voltage value, and the area of the ICA. The results of this analysis have shown the best predictive capabilities of the Feedforward Neural Network for both datasets, which not only displays one of the lowest error, but also the smallest processing time which is a significant indicator from a company point of view. In the Oxford dataset where the data does not contain outliers, the simple data-driven models such as the Tree and Ensemble Trees obtain low errors, while for the NASA dataset which contains outliers and spikes in the State of Health during the cycles, those models could not yield the same results. On the contrary, probabilistic techniques such as the Gaussian Process Regression and ML techniques such as the Convolutional Neural Network, the Nonlinear AutoRegressive network with exogenous inputs, and the Long-Short Term Memory Recurrent Neural Network show constant behavior between the two datasets, with a slight increment be due to less training data in the NASA dataset. This comparison may be exploited as a reference guide in order to choose the most suitable model, according to a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency. As a further development, the range of the tunable hyperparameters could be increased while more datasets could be tested. Furthermore, the remaining useful life is an important indicator for the LIB and is connected with the State of Health, and as a consequence, it could be estimated as well to compare the models.

Panagiotis Eleftheriadis: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Manfredi Gangi: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sonia Leva: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Alberto Valdes Rey: Data curation, Software, Validation. Emanuele Groppo: Data curation, Software, Validation. Lorenzo Grande: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
